confirmed.title: buildHeuristicSql default branch returns an unpinned recent-sessions listing and synthesis then fabricates 'data not in dataset' claims contradicting the app's own 0.99-confidence session resolution
confirmed.evidence:
All five captures share the identical fallback SQL 'SELECT session_key, session_name, date_start, year, country_name, location FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 25' (queries.ts:345-350), which ignores the session the resolver had already pinned at 0.99 confidence (M06=9987 Imola, M19=9896 Singapore, s101=10002 Suzuka, s202=9839 Abu Dhabi, s303=9892 Singapore — all in generationNotes). Rows are exclusively year=2026 (Mexico City, Yas Marina, Lusail, Las Vegas, São Paulo) vs manifest venues/year 2025. Answers then assert falsehoods: M06 'the session listing query did not return a 2025 Imola session... suggesting the data may not yet be ingested'; s202:stint_delta 'The available data does not contain Abu Dhabi 2025' — while M06's own runtime.completeness shows raw.position_history 'usable' for the session and fallbackOptions says 'infer position changes from raw.position_history'. The generationNotes pin check downgrades to 'session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate' because the fallback SQL has no session_key literal (orchestration.ts:281-288) but execution proceeds anyway.
---
buildHeuristicSql's default branch (src/lib/queries.ts:345-350) returns 'SELECT session_key... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25' — 25 late-2026 sessions unrelated to the question. s202_stint_delta answer: 'The available data does not contain Abu Dhabi 2025 — the most recent Abu Dhabi race in the dataset is session_key 11436, dated December 2026' and insight card titled 'Abu Dhabi 2025 Stint Deltas — Data Not Found', while runtime.resolution.selectedSession = 9839 / 'Race / United Arab Emirates / Yas Island / 2025' at confidence 0.99. M06: 'the session listing query did not return a 2025 Imola session in its top 25 results, suggesting the data may not yet be ingested' (session 9987 was pinned). Both sector_dominance captures claim the venue's 2025 qualifying data is absent (re-fire proved it exists: 3 minisector rows, Russell/Leclerc sector times). generationNotes even records 'session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate(session_key=...)' — the guard detects the fallback SQL ignores the pinned session, but the response still ships as a normal graded-B answer instead of an honest failure. The Did-question s202_stint_delta also ships without insight.verdict.
---
M08.json and M16.json final SQL is 'SELECT session_key, session_name, date_start, year, country_name, location FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 25' - all 25 rows are year=2026 (Yas Marina etc.). M08 answer: 'The available data does not include a Spa 2025 session... no Belgian Grand Prix at Spa-Francorchamps for 2025 appears' even though entity_resolution had already pinned session_key=9939 (Spa 2025 Race) and the insight card title says 'Mercedes Split Strategy - Spa 2025 / Belgian GP - Race - 2025', directly contradicting the answer. M16 similarly claims 'no Silverstone 2025 session appears' with session_key=9947 pinned. generationNotes also degrade to session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate because the fallback SQL has no session_key literal (orchestration.ts:286).
---
M08.json answer: 'The available data does not include a Spa 2025 session. The most recent sessions in the dataset are from the 2026 season...' — yet runtime.resolution.selectedSession = {sessionKey 9939, 'Race / Belgium / Spa-Francorchamps / 2025', confidence 0.99}. M16.json answer: 'no Silverstone 2025 session appears in the results (the most recent data shown is from late 2026...)' — yet resolution pinned sessionKey 9947 'Race / United Kingdom / Silverstone / 2025' at 0.99. In both cases the executed SQL was the heuristic default 'SELECT session_key... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 25' (25 most-recent = 2026 sessions only), and generationNotes flags 'session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate'. The synthesis layer inferred GLOBAL dataset absence from a recency-limited 25-row list, directly contradicting resolution state it had available. This is materially worse than an honest refusal: it tells the user real data does not exist.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: orchestration.ts lines 1292-1305 unconditionally swap failed template SQL for buildHeuristicSql output; for sector/stint/overtake phrasings no heuristic branch matches, so the context-free session-listing catch-all runs. Phase 17-D gave the anthropic path an honest structured failure for non-timeout errors, but the deterministic_template branch has no equivalent guard, and nothing blocks executing fallback SQL that dropped the pinned session — producing confident false 'not ingested' refusals for sessions that exist.

Answer claims 'no Spa/Silverstone 2025 session exists' while the app's own resolver had pinned that exact session at 0.99 confidence
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Fully reproduced in all 7 cited captures (M06, M08, M16, M19, s101/s303 sector_dominance, s202 stint_delta): each shares the identical unpinned fallback SQL 'SELECT session_key... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25', rows are exclusively late-2026 sessions, generationNotes record the resolver's 0.99-confidence pinned session (9987/9939/9947/9896/10002/9839/9892) plus 'session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate', and the answers assert the 2025 events are absent from the dataset ('The available data does not contain Abu Dhabi 2025', 'suggesting the data may not yet be ingested'). Code confirms: default branch of buildHeuristicSql at web/src/lib/queries.ts:345-350 returns the recent-sessions listing ignoring resolvedContext; enforcePinnedSessionKeyInSql at web/src/app/api/chat/orchestration.ts:281-288 only annotates when no session_key literal exists and execution proceeds. Design lens: heuristic-after-template-failure is a designed Phase 17-D path, and honest refusals are signed off ONLY when data is genuinely absent — here the sessions exist (resolver pinned them; M06 completeness shows usable fallback tables; sector data proven present on re-fire), so the 'not in dataset' claims are fabrications, not honest refusals. P0: confidently false statements about data availability that directly contradict the app's own resolution, shipped as graded answers across many prompts.

confirmed.title: core.grid_vs_finish finish_position stale/corrupt when raw.session_result is missing; insight builders trust it unconditionally with no final-lap-position fallback, so cards contradict their own trace
confirmed.evidence:
R03 Silverstone 9947 (capture_0/R03.json): NO driver has finish_position=1 — insight metric Winner='n/a' and the answer omits the winner sentence entirely; Norris has grid=3, finish_position=3 (his real quali slot) while his own trace in the same rows holds P1 from lap 44 to the flag; duplicate finish positions: P3 (Norris+Hulkenberg), P4 (Albon+Hamilton), P12 (Sainz+Russell). s101 Sao Paulo 9869 (capture_0/s101_position_changes#1.json): Verstappen finish_position=16 (= his real quali position) vs his own trace at P3 on lap 63 held to the flag; answer says 'Verstappen was the biggest mover, climbing from P19 to P16' when the trace shows P19 to P3 (+16); Ocon finish=17 vs trace P12 at lap 70; duplicate finishes P17 (Tsunoda+Ocon) and P18 (Stroll+Hamilton). s303 Singapore 9896 is clean (zero mismatches). Overlaps the known raw.session_result ingestion gap, but the NEW aspects are: duplicated finish positions across drivers, quali values leaking into finish_position, the missing-winner symptom, and positionChangesInsight.ts (lines 39-57, 96-101) deriving climber/faller/winner solely from finish_position without cross-checking the final trace position it already holds in the same rows.
---
R03 (Silverstone 2025) insight metrics: {'label':'Winner','value':'n/a'} — yet the position_changes spec's final-lap values show Lando NORRIS = 1, and the climber/faller metrics in the same card are happily derived from the position feed (Hulkenberg P19→P3). positionChangesInsight.ts:57 finds the winner only via finish_position (`classified.find((d) => d.finish === 1)`), which is null for this session (known raw.session_result ingestion gap), with no fallback to the last-lap position the card already renders. São Paulo and Singapore captures (s101/s303) fill the Winner tile fine because their finish_position is ingested. New consumer-side aspect of the known session_result gap.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: core.grid_vs_finish falls back to a stale/qualifying classification when raw.session_result is missing for a session, producing duplicated and wrong finish positions; the race_position_changes template and insight builder trust finish_position unconditionally, so the card self-contradicts (chart traces end at the true position while labels/headline stats use the corrupt one).

Winner metric shows 'n/a' while the same card's chart shows Norris P1 at the flag
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Fully reproduced from capture_0/R03.json and capture_0/s101_position_changes#1.json. R03: duplicate finish_position values {3:2, 4:2, 12:2}, zero drivers with finish_position=1, Winner metric 'n/a', Norris grid=3/finish=3 while his own trace holds P1 from lap 44. s101: Verstappen finish_position=16 vs trace P3 at lap 63 (position feed logs changes only, so P3 held to flag); answer asserts 'Verstappen was the biggest mover, climbing from P19 to P16' - confidently wrong; duplicates {17:2, 18:2}. s303 clean as claimed. positionChangesInsight.ts:57 derives winner solely via classified.find(d => d.finish === 1) and lines 54-56 derive climber/faller from finish_position with no cross-check against the last-lap trace it already holds. Root cause nuance: sql/007_semantic_summary_contracts.sql:47-55 shows finish_fallback = last raw.position_history row per driver (not quali leak), which explains stale/duplicated values when session_result is missing - the fallback is deliberate, but the finding's new aspects (non-null WRONG values, self-contradicting card, no consumer-side fallback to final trace position) go beyond the ticketed raw.session_result ingestion gap and are not signed off anywhere. Severity adjusted P1->P0: the card states factually wrong race results (wrong biggest-mover destination, missing winner) in plausible-looking prose and metrics.

confirmed.title: analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace aggregates lap counts over duplicated laps_enriched rows without dedup, producing physically impossible clean-air/traffic lap counts with no downstream plausibility check
confirmed.evidence:
M13.json (session 9839, Mexico City GP 2025 race, Norris): the single row has clean_air_laps="84", traffic_laps="24" — sum 108 laps, but the 2025 Mexico City GP race distance is 71 laps, so 84 clean-air laps is impossible on its face. The answer states '84 laps in clean air ... 78% of his total laps' as a headline fact. 108 = 2 x 54 is consistent with the known 2x row duplication in the upstream lap views propagating un-deduplicated into the analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace counts. Pace averages (88.183s clean vs 90.039s traffic, delta 1.855s) are internally consistent with the row and plausibly unaffected (duplication cancels in means but doubles counts). Secondary: the prompt asked about laps 'during his winning stint' and the answer silently substitutes whole-race scope.
---
M13 row (analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace, session 9839, driver 4): clean_air_laps='84', traffic_laps='24' — 108 total laps for Norris in the Mexico City GP (~71 laps; no F1 race exceeds 78). The stacked_horizontal_bar renders 84 vs 24 and the answer asserts '84 laps in clean air... 78% of his total laps was a key factor in his race victory' with no caveat. For comparison, the Zandvoort race trace in the same capture set spans 72 laps. This looks like the warehouse dup-row artifact (core matviews ship duplicate rows) propagating into analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace COUNTs — a new user-facing surface for it. Secondary: the question asked about 'his winning stint' but the matview is session-grain, and the answer silently reframes to the whole race.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace warehouse view counts laps over the duplicated laps_enriched rows without dedup, doubling clean_air_laps/traffic_laps; the LLM path (generationNotes: 'Using analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace ... clean_air_laps ... directly') and answer synthesis have no plausibility check against race distance. New aspect of the known dup-rows-in-core-matviews item: it extends to the analytics schema's counted columns and reaches user-facing answer text unchecked.
member_titles: M13 answer claims 84 clean-air laps in a 71-lap race — analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace lap counts physically impossible
Clean-air card shows physically impossible lap counts: 108 laps (84 clean + 24 traffic) for one driver in a ~71-lap Mexico City race
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced from capture_3/M13.json: single row from analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace (session_key=9839, driver 4) with clean_air_laps='84', traffic_laps='24' (108 total); the answer headlines '84 laps in clean air... 78% of his total laps was a key factor in his race victory' and the insight says '84 of 108 total laps (78%)' with no caveat. One correction to the finding's framing: runtime.resolution.selectedSession labels 9839 as 'Race / United Arab Emirates / Yas Marina / 2025' (58-lap race), not Mexico City — the auditor echoed the app's own mistaken genNote venue; the physical impossibility holds regardless since no F1 race exceeds ~78 laps, and 108 = 2x54 is consistent with the known 2x duplication propagating into counted columns while means cancel (pace values are internally plausible). The secondary claim also verifies: the prompt asked about the winning stint and the answer silently reframes to whole-race scope. Design lens: dup rows in core matviews is a known open item, but this is a genuinely new aspect — propagation into analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace COUNT columns reaching user-facing answer text; grep shows no dedup handling, plausibility check, or ticket for this surface (schemaCatalog/anthropic prompt/health-check questions all use the view uncritically). Adjusted to P0: the headline number answering the user's exact question ('how many laps in clean air') is roughly double reality and physically impossible — a confidently wrong user-facing answer, not mere quality erosion.

confirmed.title: Deterministic template SQL exceeds the 15s Neon statement timeout on unmaterialized core.* views (multi-scan CTEs, telemetry joins) under cold cache/concurrency
confirmed.evidence:
single_driver_pit_cycle succeeded for Baku (capture_0/s202_pit_stop#1.json) at 13368ms total — near the ~15s statement budget known for un-materialized core.* views — but the identical template failed at exec for Canada 9963 (M22, 41635ms total = failed template attempt + heuristic + synthesis). driver_pair_stint_delta failed for Bahrain 10014 (M10, 66569ms total). pitCycle.ts builds TWO separate MATERIALIZED scans of core.laps_enriched (laps CTE lines 107-118 and venue CTE lines 119-127); stintDelta.ts scans laps_enriched twice (a_laps/b_laps CTEs, lines 76-98) plus raw.stints joins. The captures do not carry the exec error text, so a raw.stints schema mismatch in stintDelta is a secondary hypothesis; the timing pattern favors intermittent statement timeout.
---
8/17 captures have generationSource='heuristic_after_template_failure' (5 are randomized entries or R04 baseline which MUST be deterministic_template). /Users/robertzehnder/Documents/coding/f1/openf1/web/logs/chat_api.log shows chat_query_first_attempt_failed with error 'canceling statement due to statement timeout' for every one: 04:41:59.786Z R04 (driver_telemetry_overlay, session 10006), 04:42:36.180Z M06 (inferred_overtakes, 9987), 04:43:20.282Z M19 (inferred_overtakes, 9896), 04:43:59.215Z s101 telemetry (9888), 04:44:40.399Z s101 sector_dominance (10002), 04:45:13.529Z s202 stint_delta (9839), 04:45:55.055Z s303 telemetry (9869), 04:46:32.882Z s303 sector_dominance (9892). Timeout is SET LOCAL statement_timeout = OPENF1_QUERY_TIMEOUT_MS default 15000 in queries/execute.ts. Succeeding templates already run 7-12.9s (s101 race_trace result.elapsedMs=12008, M02=12896), i.e. within ~20% of the same budget.
---
6/17 captures show generationSource=heuristic_after_template_failure with the correct template named first (driver_pair_strategy_split, driver_pair_sector_dominance, single_driver_pit_cycle x2, single_driver_speed_map x2). Executor sets SET LOCAL statement_timeout=15000 (web/src/lib/queries/execute.ts, OPENF1_QUERY_TIMEOUT_MS=15000 in web/.env.local). Every template that SUCCEEDED in the run took 7.5-12.9s of SQL exec (response.result.elapsedMs: s101_strategy_split 12028ms, s202_position_changes 12447ms, R01 12895ms, M20 10629ms, M12 9375ms) - almost no headroom. pitCycle.ts scans core.laps_enriched in two separate CTEs (laps + venue) and speedMap.ts joins raw.car_data over a lap-timestamp window; both failed in BOTH s101 and s303 even after warm-up (captures at 00:44-00:47), and even the trivial single-scan heuristic replacement on laps_enriched took 10610ms (s101_pit_stop) / 5626ms (s303_pit_stop). strategy_split/sector_dominance failed only in the cold window (M08/M16 at 00:42-00:43, when R01 stage logs show entity_resolution=18549ms and completeness_check=17649ms) and the identical strategy_split SQL succeeded at 00:43+ for sessions 9877/9920. Elapsed totals of the failing captures (20.4s-44.5s with final SQL exec of only 0.06-12s) fit a 15s template timeout plus fallback plus synthesis. Known item 'core.* relations are all unmaterialized views' covered probes only; this is a new aspect: the shipped template SQL itself blows the same budget.
---
logs/chat_api.log has 23 'canceling statement due to statement timeout' chat_query_first_attempt_failed entries on 2026-07-02 (04:41:57Z-04:46:34Z, exactly the capture_2 window; capture files mtime Jul 2 00:42-00:47 EDT). The failed SQL matches the template builders verbatim: 'WITH pits AS MATERIALIZED' (single_driver_pit_cycle, x3), 'WITH fastest AS' (single_driver_speed_map, x2), 'WITH stints AS SELECT DISTINCT' (driver_pair_strategy_split), 'WITH a AS (SELECT MIN(duration_sector_1)...' Pool statement_timeout defaults to 15000ms (OPENF1_QUERY_TIMEOUT_MS, db/driver.ts:51). confirmed.title: orchestration.ts deterministic_template failure branch swaps in buildHeuristicSql for ANY exec error — no timeout/permanent distinction, no retry, no topical-relevance check, honest-refusal path unreachable
confirmed.evidence:
M10 (capture_0/M10.json): generationSource=heuristic_after_template_failure, notes 'template=driver_pair_stint_delta | template_failed_heuristic_used | session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate(session_key=10014)'. Executed SQL is buildHeuristicSql's DEFAULT branch — 'SELECT session_key, session_name, date_start, year, country_name, location FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25' — returning 25 rows with year=2026 and venues Las Vegas/Lusail/Mexico City/Sao Paulo/Yas Marina for a Bahrain-2025 Hamilton-vs-Leclerc stint question; the answer admits no stint data is available; requested drivers absent from rows; elapsed 66569ms; self-grader still gave adequacyGrade=B. M22 (capture_0/M22.json): 'template=single_driver_pit_cycle | template_failed_heuristic_used'; heuristic 'lap' branch returned 140 rows = 70 laps x exact 2x duplicates, no venue/year columns. orchestration.ts lines 1292-1305 call buildHeuristicSql on ANY deterministic-template exec failure with no error-class split, unlike the Phase 17-D anthropic path (lines 1264-1289: timeout keeps heuristic, other errors get honest structured failure); the comment at orchestration.ts:1075 says the 'recent sessions' heuristic was supposed to be retired.
---
M10 (expect stint_delta_line): generationNotes 'template=driver_pair_stint_delta | template_failed_heuristic_used'; fallback SQL is 'SELECT session_key... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25' returning 25 rows of 2026 Yas Marina sessions for a Bahrain-2025 Hamilton/Leclerc question; no detector fires, no chart, and the insight is still titled 'Hamilton vs Leclerc Stint Deltas — Bahrain 2025' over a sessions list. M22 (expect pit_event_strip): 'template=single_driver_pit_cycle | template_failed_heuristic_used'; fallback dumps 140 lap-duration rows (session 9963, driver 1) with no pit columns, so no pit_event_strip is possible and the answer infers the stop lap from sector-3 inflation. Same template succeeded for Bearman/Baku (s202_pit_stop#1, 13.4s) — failure is per-request, elapsedMs 66569 (M10) / 41635 (M22) consistent with statement timeouts. orchestration.ts:1292-1305 routes ANY deterministic-template exec error to buildHeuristicSql, unlike the anthropic branch which distinguishes timeout vs column errors (lines 1264-1289).

All 8 captures carry generationNotes 'template_failed_heuristic_used'. web/logs/chat_api.log chat_query_first_attempt_failed entries for their requestIds (9712bbaa, 5903843c, 4a18755c, d260c3be, 5562b9ab, 2bbc5028-adjacent set, 9ae131eb, 0068cc18) all show error 'canceling statement due to statement timeout' on the original template SQL (driver_pair_stint_delta, driver_pair_sector_dominance, driver_telemetry_overlay, inferred_overtakes). statement_timeout defaults to 15000ms (src/lib/db/driver.ts:51-55). Re-firing the identical s101_sector_dominance prompt against the warm server succeeded as deterministic_template (17.1s wall, 3 rows, track_heatmap spec emitted and verified) — the timeouts are transient, yet orchestration.ts:1292-1305 sends ANY deterministic-template exec error (including timeouts) straight to buildHeuristicSql with no retry, unlike the LLM path which gets one repair attempt. Result: expected charts (track_heatmap, telemetry_overlay, stint_delta_line, horizontal_bar) never render; detector registry returns none for all 8.

The heuristic rows are unrelated to the question: s101_stint_delta#1 and s202_sector_dominance#1 got `SELECT session_key... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25` returning rows[0]={session_key:11436, year:2026, location:'Yas Marina'} for Suzuka-2025 / Mexico-City-2025 questions; s101/s303_lap1_launch and s202_telemetry_overlay got single-driver `core.laps_enriched` lap durations (driver 27 / 63 / 87 only) for two-driver questions; M04 got 200 all-zero raw.car_data samples (speed/throttle/brake/rpm all 0). The anthropic path (orchestration.ts:1281-1289) fails honestly on non-timeout errors; the deterministic_template branch (orchestration.ts:1292-1305) ALWAYS runs buildHeuristicSql, whose final default branch is a bare sessions listing (src/lib/queries.ts:153+).
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: The deterministic_template failure branch in orchestration.ts never distinguishes timeout from permanent failure and never checks that the heuristic SQL is even topically related; buildHeuristicSql's default branch is a generic recent-sessions list, so any template exec failure on an analysis question degrades to unrelated 2026 data instead of an honest structured failure.

confirmed.title: Non-template SQL paths (buildHeuristicSql laps branch, LLM SQL prompt) never got the template-standard laps_enriched GROUP BY/DISTINCT dedup or venue/year projection — exact 2x duplicate rows and unverifiable results
confirmed.evidence:
M22 heuristic SQL (queries.ts lines 318-325): 'SELECT lap_number, lap_duration, duration_sector_1, duration_sector_2, duration_sector_3 FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = 9963 AND driver_number = 1 ORDER BY lap_number ASC' — result is 140 rows for a 70-lap race, every lap present exactly twice with identical values (verified all 70 dup pairs byte-identical), and the final SELECT carries no venue/year columns so downstream venue verification is impossible. M01 (LLM path, capture_0/M01.json): 'FROM core.laps_enriched le WHERE le.session_key = 10002 ... ORDER BY le.lap_duration ASC LIMIT 1' with no DISTINCT/GROUP BY — benign here only because LIMIT 1 with no aggregate, but any COUNT/AVG/SUM would silently double. anthropic.ts's system prompt mentions core.laps_enriched as the default lap contract (lines 86, 114, 127) but contains zero duplicate-row/dedup guidance, while every deterministic template explicitly guards (e.g. stintDelta.ts lapsCte comment 'laps_enriched ships duplicate rows in the warehouse, so collapse to one row per lap').
---
M22's heuristic SQL 'SELECT lap_number, lap_duration, ... FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = 9963 AND driver_number = 1 ORDER BY lap_number ASC' returned 140 rows for a 70-lap race — every lap duplicated exactly 2x with identical values (verified programmatically: all lap_number counts == 2). pitCycle.ts:22-24 documents the gotcha ('core.laps_enriched carries duplicate rows per (session, driver, lap) in the deployed warehouse') and every deterministic template de-dupes with GROUP BY/DISTINCT, but buildHeuristicSql emits bare SELECTs. New aspect of the known dup-row gotcha: the fallback path re-exposes it, doubling row counts fed to the LLM/answer and to any downstream detector aggregation.
---
All three share the fallback SQL 'SELECT lap_number, lap_duration, duration_sector_1, duration_sector_2, duration_sector_3 FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = <k> AND driver_number = <n> ORDER BY lap_number ASC' (queries.ts:319-323). (1) Single driver only: R04 driver_number=1 (Verstappen) with Norris absent; s101 =10 (Gasly) with Hulkenberg absent; s303 =31 (Ocon) with Gasly absent — each answer admits it ('Norris's data was not included in the SQL results'). (2) No GROUP BY/DISTINCT on core.laps_enriched, hitting the known Neon exact-2x duplication: 106 rows = 53 laps x2 (R04), 110 = 55x2 (s101), 142 = 71x2 (s303) — every lap_number appears twice with identical values. (3) Final SELECT carries no venue/year/driver columns, so downstream venue verification and the drivers-in-rows resolution check are impossible. Manifest expect=telemetry_overlay is never produced; insight has no chart.
---
Fallback SQL: 'SELECT lap_number, lap_duration, duration_sector_1, duration_sector_2, duration_sector_3 FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = 9912 AND driver_number = 4 ORDER BY lap_number ASC' (web/src/lib/queries.ts ~line 317). s101_pit_stop returned 106 rows = 53 laps each duplicated exactly (lap 1 appears twice with identical values); s303 returned 100 rows = 50 laps x2 - the known Neon laps_enriched 2x duplication with no dedup. The final SELECT also carries no venue/year columns (venue verification impossible) and no driver_name (manifest drivers Norris/Piastri absent from rows). Consequence: the answer never states the first-stop lap - 'that information is present in the full 100-row result but not visible in the provided sample' - because duplication halves the row sample the synthesizer sees (it saw only laps 1-13). Norris's Monza stop lap IS derivable from the returned 53 laps.
---
M03.json (anthropic_repaired, allowed LLM baseline): final SELECT returns only corner_label/corner_number/braking metrics from analytics.braking_performance with no country_name/location/year despite the pinned session 10022 - the only capture in the set without venue/year columns on a successful path (all deterministic templates append them via a sess CTE). The single returned row therefore cannot be cross-checked against the manifest venue (Saudi Arabia), and downstream venue verification over rows has nothing to read. Answer content itself checks out against the row (311/53/258 kph, 88.3% avg pressure, 50 valid laps).
---
s101:lap1_launch#1 returned 88 rows = 44 laps x2 (44 exact-duplicate row groups, e.g. lap_number 1 twice with identical lap_duration 206.946); s303:lap1_launch#1 104 = 52x2; s202:telemetry_overlay#1 142 = 71x2 (Bearman answer even says 'the data covers 142 rows (with duplicates)'). SQL at queries.ts:318-323 has no GROUP BY/DISTINCT despite the documented Neon 2x-duplication in core.laps_enriched. M09's LLM-generated SQL has the same flaw in its stint_laps CTE (per-lap SELECT from laps_enriched with no dedup) — it never executed, but would have returned doubled rows. All deterministic templates dedup correctly (e.g. stintDelta.ts a_laps GROUP BY lap_number with the comment 'laps_enriched ships duplicate rows in the warehouse').

confirmed.title: buildHeuristicSql is a keyword-first router that ignores resolved context — cannot express driver pairs, 'fastest' branch drops the asked driver, emitting degenerate off-topic canned queries
confirmed.evidence:
buildHeuristicSql's context parameter is singular ({sessionKey?, driverNumber?}, src/lib/queries.ts:153-156); the lap/pace branch (queries.ts:316-324) emits 'WHERE session_key = X AND driver_number = N' for exactly one driver even though runtime resolution selected both (R04: session 10006 drivers 1+4 resolved, SQL has only driver_number = 1; s101: 9888 drivers 10+27, SQL only 10; s303: 9869 drivers 31+10, SQL only 31). Rows carry no driver identity columns (lap_number, lap_duration, 3 sector durations), so the detector registry emits NO spec — expected telemetry_overlay never renders, and insight metrics describe only one driver of a two-driver comparison. The backing /api/lap-telemetry endpoint works fine for these exact params (curled sessionKey=10006&drivers=1,4 → 200, both drivers, 346/342 samples; 9888&drivers=10,27 → 200; 9869&drivers=31,10 → 200).

Both speed_map fallbacks ran the 'fastest' branch (web/src/lib/queries.ts ~line 163): top-5 best_lap_duration per session with LIMIT 5, no driver_number predicate even though context.driverNumber was resolved (stage logs: Ocon #31 for 9904, Sainz #55 for 9971). Rows contain Verstappen/Russell/Hadjar... - manifest drivers Ocon and Sainz are absent, failing the resolution check. Answers punt: 'he does not appear among the five fastest drivers... a dedicated telemetry or car-data query filtered to Ocon's driver number would' [be needed]. Expected chart track_speed_map is lost entirely.

(a) Speed-map prompts contain 'fastest', so they hit the first branch (queries.ts:163-181) which builds a session-wide top-5 best-lap leaderboard and IGNORES the resolved driverNumber entirely — Ocon (#31, resolved 0.85 confidence) absent from s101:speed_map rows, Sainz (#55) absent from s303:speed_map rows; the detector then renders a horizontal_bar of five OTHER drivers' best laps under a 'show Ocon's speed map' ask (render degradation: expected track_speed_map, got horizontal_bar of wrong entities). (b) Pit-stop prompts hit the lap/pace branch (queries.ts:317-323): raw per-lap durations from core.laps_enriched with no pit columns and no dedup — s101:pit_stop returned 106 rows for 53 unique laps, every lap duplicated x2 (known warehouse dup-row gotcha, unguarded here), and no detector fires (expected pit_event_strip, got none). (c) Strategy-split/sector-dominance wording matches no keyword branch and falls to the default (queries.ts:345-350): 'FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25' with no session_key predicate despite context.sessionKey being available — flagged by the app itself as session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate.
---
Three degenerate shapes, all from buildHeuristicSql (queries.ts:153-351): (1) s101:stint_delta#1 and s202:sector_dominance#1 fell to the final catch-all 'SELECT ... FROM core.sessions ORDER BY date_start DESC LIMIT 25' — 25 rows, ALL year=2026 Yas Marina sessions vs manifest venues Suzuka/Mexico City; zero prompt-named drivers in rows; no session_key predicate (generationNotes: session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate). (2) s101/s303 lap1_launch and s202:telemetry_overlay matched the 'lap|pace' branch (queries.ts:316-323) — single-driver lap dump: only Hulkenberg for a Hulkenberg-vs-Russell positions question, only Russell for Russell-vs-Leclerc, only Bearman for Bearman-vs-Piastri; no position/grid columns for a positions-gained question; no venue/year columns in the SELECT (breaks downstream venue verification). (3) M04 matched the telemetry branch via the substring 'speed' inside 'high-speed' — raw.car_data ORDER BY date ASC LIMIT 2000 returned the first 200 samples (exact preview-cap truncation, rowCount===200) of pre-session garage telemetry: every speed/throttle/brake/rpm value is 0. Orchestration's own comment (orchestration.ts:1282-1284) states failures should be 'honest structured failure, NOT a heuristic that returns unrelated rows', but that policy is only applied on the anthropic path.

confirmed.title: Budget accounting charges pre-SQL resolution latency against the SQL pipeline deadline, leaving zero budget for the designed repair/fallback path; raw error surfaces with a double-concatenated sentence
confirmed.evidence: M09.json: generationSource=sql_generation_failed, sqlError 'I couldn't construct a valid SQL query for this question within the time budget' (code sql_repair_timeout), no result rows, elapsedMs=69,837. Because sqlPipelineDeadline had already passed, orchestration.ts:1247-1252 gave up before the repair attempt AND before the heuristic_after_sql_timeout branch (lines 1264-1280) that is specifically designed for this timeout class. The final answer duplicates itself — 'I couldn't construct a valid SQL query for this question. I couldn't construct a valid SQL query for this question within the time budget.' — because line 1337 prepends the generic prefix to a sqlErrorDetail that already contains the same sentence. confirmed.title: compound_degradation_curve template SQL has no SC/VSC/red-flag or standing-start exclusion, so fresh-tyre baselines and per-age medians are contaminated at disrupted races, inverting curves and blowing out axes
confirmed.evidence:
M11 medium rows: age 1 delta +2.788s, age 2 +8.204s, then ages 3-15 all ~ -32.2 to -32.8s, with lap_count=2 at every age — i.e. the age<=2 'fresh baseline' laps were ~32s slower than genuine pace (SC/opening-lap running at Jeddah), so every later lap reads 32s 'faster'. The answer states 'Medium crosses the cliff threshold around age 1 and stays high (-32.23s by age 15)' — literally contradictory: a compound that is 32s under its baseline did not stay high. Cliff logic in degradationCurveInsight.ts:66-73 declares a sustained cliff if a point exceeds 0.6s AND only the SINGLE next point exceeds 0.4s (age1=2.788, age2=8.204 qualify), never re-checking the remaining 13 points; the template SQL (degradationCurve.ts:50-60) filters is_valid/is_pit_lap/is_pit_out_lap but not neutralized laps (unlike raceTrace, which computes is_neutralized), and there is no minimum-sample guard (n=2 laps per point here). The s202 Mexico City capture shows the same code behaving sensibly when baselines are clean, confirming the defect is baseline/detector robustness, not arithmetic.
---
M11 (Jeddah, session 10022) rows: HARD compound_baseline_s = 136.412 while deltas from age 3 onward sit at -41.3..-43.8s (i.e. real race pace ~93s — the age<=2 baseline laps ran ~44s slow, consistent with an early SC/standing start); MEDIUM baseline 125.462 with -32s deltas. The emitted degradation_curve spec plots these as the y-series, and the answer states 'Hard nets out 1.511s/lap FASTER with age (-43.82s by age 30) — fuel burn-off outweighs tyre wear here', a physically impossible claim delivered without hedging. s202 (Mexico City) shows the milder form: tyre_age 0 buckets at +11.66/+9.23/+11.11s (standing-start lap 1 included) dominating the chart's y-scale. Template SQL (degradationCurve.ts) filters is_pit_lap/is_pit_out_lap/is_valid but has no neutralized-lap or lap_number>1 exclusion in either the baselines CTE (WHERE tyre_age <= 2) or the per-age buckets, and lap_count>=2 admits 2-lap buckets.
---
Bahrain (R02 / s303, session 10014): rows MEDIUM tyre_age=0 deg_delta_s=+8.002 (5 laps) and SOFT age=0 +6.616 (7 laps) vs a real signal inside ±3s — the age-0 laps are race-start laps (tyre_age 0, not pit-out), so the rendered line starts at +8s and the auto-scaled y-domain squashes the actual deg trend. Monaco (s101, session 9979): detector series Hard=[-4.696, 12.786, 8.707, -21.05, -21.952, ...], Medium=[-5.906, 13.381, -14.849, ...] — swings of 35s because the fresh (age<=2) baseline itself was set under a disruption; the chart renders a +/-22s 'tyre cliff' curve that is pure race-disruption noise. The template (degradationCurve.ts:52-60) filters is_valid/is_pit_lap/is_pit_out_lap only; raceTrace.ts:97 already infers is_neutralized from synchronized lap-time spikes but that technique is not reused here, and lap 1 is never excluded.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: Two compounding gaps: (1) the compound baseline (median of age<=2 laps) includes safety-car-paced laps because the template has no is_neutralized/outlier filter and no minimum lap-count threshold; (2) the cliff detector confirms 'sustained' with only one look-ahead point and the takeaway template prints 'stays high' alongside a negative endpoint without a sign check.
member_titles: Deg-curve fresh-tyre baseline is polluted by neutralized/SC laps and the cliff detector then emits a self-contradictory claim ('crosses the cliff... stays high (-32.23s)')
Compound-deg fresh-tyre baseline (tyre_age<=2 median) has no SC/standing-start exclusion — Jeddah curves show compounds 'improving' 32-43s and prose claims 1.5s/lap fuel gain
Degradation-curve template medians are contaminated by lap-1 standing starts and SC/red-flag laps, blowing out the y-axis and inverting the curve at disrupted races
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced across all five captures. M11 (Jeddah 10022): medium age1 +2.788/age2 +8.204 then ages 3-15 at ~-32.2..-32.8 with lap_count=2 at every age; hard baseline 136.412 with -41..-43.8 deltas; answer asserts 'Hard nets out 1.511s/lap FASTER with age (-43.82s by age 30) - fuel burn-off outweighs tyre wear here' and 'Medium crosses the cliff threshold around age 1 and stays high (-32.23s by age 15)' - internally contradictory. R02/s303 Sakhir age-0 buckets +6.616/+8.002; s202 Mexico age-0 +11.66/+9.23/+11.11; s101 Monaco swings (12.786 to -21.95) all match. Code confirms: degradationCurve.ts filters only is_valid/is_pit_lap/is_pit_out_lap with no neutralized-lap or lap-1 exclusion, baselines CTE is median of age<=2 laps with no sample-size guard, HAVING lap_count>=2 admits 2-lap buckets; degradationCurveInsight.ts:69 confirms cliff with a single look-ahead point; raceTrace.ts:97 computes is_neutralized but the technique is unreused here. One overstatement: the M11 answer DOES carry a hedge ('curves are NOT fuel-corrected... net slopes understate (or invert) true compound deg'), so 'delivered without hedging' is not strictly accurate - though attributing a 43s delta to fuel burn remains wrong. Design lens: the signed-off 'SC windows inferred from lap-time spikes' decision covers the race-trace card's stated model, not deg-curve baseline contamination; no test or RUBRIC entry sanctions this. P1 stands (borderline P0): wrong analysis at disrupted races, partially mitigated by the printed caveat and visually self-evident chart breakage; clean races behave correctly.

confirmed.title: radarDetector builds axes from all numeric non-identifier columns and IDENTIFIER_COLS misses season_year, so 2025 is plotted as an axis on a 0-100 radar and axis counts contradict the card text
confirmed.evidence:
Both emitted specs: axes ['Season year','Restart','Traffic','Consistency'], series values [2025, 75, 18.4, 0] / [2025, 100, 0, 80], max_value 100, total_axes 8, partial_data_axes 4. The renderer (radar-chart.tsx:38-42) uses PolarRadiusAxis domain=[0,100] with no clamping, so the 'Season year' vertex is drawn at ~20x the grid radius, destroying the polygon; the caption reads '4 of 8 axes not yet populated' though the driver_performance_score matview has 7 axes. Root: IDENTIFIER_COLS (registry.ts:18-26) contains 'year' but not 'season_year', and radarDetector.build() (registry.ts:618-620) includes every numeric non-identifier column as an axis. The signed-off zero-axis dropping works as designed; this is a distinct leak of a non-score column.
---
Detector output for both radar captures: {axes:["Season year","Restart","Traffic","Consistency"], max_value:100, series:[{name:"Max VERSTAPPEN", values:[2025,75,80.1,30]},{name:"Lando NORRIS", values:[2025,100,0,60]}], partial_data_axes:4, total_axes:8}. Rows carry season_year:2025 (numeric) from the driver_pair_performance_radar template SQL. radar-chart.tsx pins PolarRadiusAxis domain=[0,100] (line 40), so 2025 spikes both polygons off-scale on the bogus 'Season year' axis. The renderer caption will read '4 of 8 axes not yet populated' while the server card (performanceRadarInsight.ts:120) says 'Season 2025 · 7-axis model · 1 axes populated' and takeaway '6 of 7 axes not yet populated' — chart and card contradict each other.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: radarDetector.build() axis selection is 'all numeric non-identifier columns' and the identifier denylist misses season_year, so the matview's season_year column becomes a 2025-valued axis on a 100-max radar.
member_titles: Radar detector leaks season_year as a polygon axis — value 2025 plotted on a 0-100 polar scale and axis-count caption inflated to 8
Radar detector charts season_year as a performance axis — value 2025 on a fixed 0-100 radius scale — and its axis counts contradict the card text
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified from rows + code (the chart spec is built client-side by mapInsight detectChart, so it is not in the captured response, but its production is deterministic from the captured rows). All four radar captures (M17, s101/s202/s303_radar) carry numeric season_year:2025 in every row alongside string-numeric *_axis columns. registry.ts IDENTIFIER_COLS (lines 18-26) contains 'year' but not 'season_year'; radarDetector.build() (lines 617-655) takes every numeric-like non-identifier column as an axis (isNumericLike accepts pg numeric strings), so for M17 cols = season_year + 7 axis columns = total_axes 8, zero-across-both axes dropped = 4, leaving axes ['Season year','Restart','Traffic','Consistency'] with values [2025,75,80.1,30]/[2025,100,0,60] — exactly the spec the finding quotes. radar-chart.tsx:38-43 pins PolarRadiusAxis domain [0,100] with no clamping, so the 2025 vertex renders ~20x off-scale. performanceRadarInsight.ts:93/120 counts only the 7 real axes ('7-axis model', 'N of 7 not yet populated'), contradicting the chart's 8/'4 of 8'. Design lens: the zero-axis dropping is the signed-off behavior and the finding correctly treats it as such; the season_year leak is a distinct oversight — 'year' being in the denylist shows the intent was to exclude identifier years, and no test or comment references season_year (grep of mapInsight/, mapInsight.test.ts finds nothing). P1 confirmed: the polygon is visually destroyed on a shipped chart shape and the chart contradicts the card text, though the textual card/answer remain correct.

confirmed.title: analytics.weather_impact lap-range stint join emits duplicate (driver, lap) rows at tyre-change boundary laps with contradictory compound/wet flags; consumers have no per-(driver,lap) de-dupe
confirmed.evidence:
M14 Melbourne 9693 (capture_0/M14.json): laps 34 and 44 appear TWICE per driver — identical lap_time_s (e.g. Norris lap 34: 122.273 in both) but one row (wet_track=0, HARD) and one (wet_track=1, INTERMEDIATE); the same lap is flagged both wet and dry. s101 (same session, Alonso/Stroll): Stroll laps 33 and 44 duplicated the same way. User-visible corruption: (a) 'laps flagged wet' differs between the two cards for the SAME race — M14 answer says 48, s101 answer says 47; (b) s101 takeaway renders phantom one-lap stints: 'Stroll tyres: Int 1-32 -> Hard 33-33 -> Int 33-33 -> Hard 34-44 -> Int 44-57 (each switch = a pit stop, marked on the chart)' — implying two pit stops on lap 33; (c) the line_dual_axis chart receives two points per duplicated lap. The template's SELECT DISTINCT (wetCrossover.ts) cannot dedup because compound_name and wet_track genuinely differ between the pair.
---
M14 rows contain TWO rows for Norris lap 34 (identical lap_time_s 122.273) — one {wet_track:0, compound:HARD}, one {wet_track:1, compound:INTERMEDIATE}; same for Piastri lap 34 and Stroll lap 33 in s101. The wetCrossover.ts template header promises 'one row per (driver, lap)' but its SELECT DISTINCT keeps both compound/wet states on the stint-boundary lap. The registry marker builder (registry.ts:888-908) treats every compound change in row order as a pit stop, producing M14 vertical_markers: ['Norris → Hard (x=34)', 'Norris → Int (x=34)', 'Norris → Hard (x=35)', ...] — a phantom Hard→Int→Hard flip-flop rendered as three red ReferenceLines with overlapping labels; identical quadruple for Piastri and for Stroll in s101. The genuine markers (lap 34 crossover, lap 44 return to inters) are real.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: The warehouse view analytics.weather_impact joins laps to stints on a lap-range condition where a pit lap falls inside BOTH the outgoing and incoming stint windows, emitting one row per (lap x stint) at every tyre change; the wetCrossover template and insight builder consume rows raw without collapsing to one row per (driver, lap).
member_titles: analytics.weather_impact emits duplicate (driver, lap) rows at stint-boundary laps with contradictory wet_track/compound; SELECT DISTINCT cannot collapse them
Crossover-lap duplicate rows create phantom pit markers (driver appears to pit 3 times in 2 laps)
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced in both captures: M14 has Norris and Piastri laps 34 and 44 each twice with identical lap_time_s but contradictory {wet_track:0, HARD} vs {wet_track:1, INTERMEDIATE}; s101 has Stroll laps 33/44 duplicated the same way. wetCrossover.ts's header promises one row per (driver, lap) but its SELECT DISTINCT cannot collapse rows whose compound/wet genuinely differ — contract violated. User-visible: key_takeaways assert phantom stints ("Norris tyres: Int 1–33 → Hard 34–34 → Int 34–34 → Hard 35–44 ... each switch = a pit stop"), i.e. two pit stops that never happened per driver, and the same-race wet-lap count differs across cards (48 vs 47). The registry's compound-transition marker builder will also render the phantom Hard→Int→Hard flip-flop as extra reference lines. The signed-off "wet-crossover model-inferred, stated on cards" decision covers inference disclaimers, not contradictory duplicate rows or fabricated pit stops. Upgraded P2→P1: affirmatively false pit-stop claims and phantom chart markers on a deterministic hero card, though the headline crossover-lap answer remains correct and the card carries an inference disclaimer.

confirmed.title: Strategy-split verdict derives from pit-lap counts alone, never checking stint coverage of race distance or retirement, so DNFs masquerade as deliberate strategy choices
confirmed.evidence:
Zandvoort 2025 (session 9920, ~72-lap race): rows show Hamilton with a single MEDIUM stint ending at lap 22 and finish_position 20, Leclerc's last stint ending at lap 53 with finish_position 19 (a 1-lap SOFT stint 3 with avg_valid_lap NULL) - both Ferraris retired. The card answers 'Hamilton ran Med (no stops). That is a genuine strategy split: Leclerc made 2 stops to Hamilton's 0.' Framing a crash-shortened race as a deliberate 0-stop strategy is factually wrong. web/src/lib/synthesis/strategySplitInsight.ts line 229 emits the verdict purely from stop-count difference; finish_position and stint-coverage-vs-race-length are available in the rows (line 98 already parses finish_position) but unused for a DNF guard.
---
Zandvoort 2025 (Ferrari Leclerc/Hamilton): rows show Hamilton's ONLY stint ends at lap 22 of total_laps 53 (stint_gantt spec: Hamilton bar 1-22 of 53; finish_position 20 = classified last after retirement). Answer: 'Hamilton ran Med (no stops). That is a genuine strategy split: Leclerc made 2 stops to Hamilton's 0.' Verdict YES. strategySplitInsight.ts:133 derives 'no stops' purely from pit-lap count and :229 declares the split; grep for retire/dnf in the file returns nothing — there is no coverage/retirement check. The builder even has a micro-stint safety-car caveat for Leclerc's 1-lap stint 3 but says nothing about the comparison driver's race ending at 42% distance, which invalidates a stop-count comparison. The gantt chart itself honestly shows the short bar, contradicting the prose.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: buildStrategySplitInsight derives the split verdict from pit-lap counts alone; it never checks whether either driver's stints cover the race distance or whether finish_position indicates retirement, so early DNFs masquerade as aggressive strategy choices.
member_titles: Strategy-split verdict counts stops without checking retirements - declares a 'genuine strategy split' when both drivers DNF'd
Strategy-split card presents a mid-race retirement as a deliberate 'no stops' strategy and returns verdict YES 'genuine strategy split'
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced from capture_2/s303_strategy_split#1.json: rows show Hamilton with a single MEDIUM stint laps 1-22, finish_position 20, and Leclerc's stints ending lap 53 (1-lap SOFT stint 3, avg_valid_lap null), finish_position 19 — both Ferraris retired at Zandvoort 2025 (~72-lap race). The answer states 'Hamilton ran Med (no stops). That is a genuine strategy split: Leclerc made 2 stops to Hamilton's 0' with verdict YES. Code confirms: web/src/lib/synthesis/strategySplitInsight.ts derives isSplit purely from stopCountsDiffer||sequencesDiffer and emits the verdict from that alone; grep for retire/dnf/coverage in the file returns nothing; finishPosition is parsed (~line 98) but only displayed as a metric tile, never used as a guard. Design lens: the test file (strategy-split-insight.test.mjs) has no DNF case and RUBRIC.md doesn't cover retirements — not designed or ticketed; the builder even added a micro-stint safety-car caveat, showing this class of caution was in scope but the DNF guard was missed. Both drivers ran identical Med openers, so the split is purely an artifact of Hamilton's retirement and the YES verdict is factually wrong, though the gantt bars and finish tiles on the same card honestly show the short stint. Adjusted to P1 (from P2): a deterministic card returns a confidently wrong verdict on the direct yes/no question whenever a compared driver retires — a common scenario — tempered below P0 by the honest contradicting data visible on the same card.

confirmed.title: Team resolution requires the message to contain the full canonical team_name ('Red Bull' never matches 'Red Bull Racing'), silently collapsing team-vs-team comparisons to one team
confirmed.evidence: M11 'Compare medium-compound deg curves between McLaren and Red Bull in stint 2 at Jeddah 2025' resolved driverCandidates = Norris (#4) + Piastri (#81) only, both matchedOn ['team_name']; no Red Bull driver was resolved. scoreDriverCandidate uses normalizedMessage.includes(teamName) (src/lib/chatRuntime/resolution.ts:292) — 'mclaren' is a substring of the message but the canonical 'red bull racing' is not ('red bull' alone doesn't satisfy the containment direction). The degradation template then ran with driver_number IN (4, 81) (both McLaren), the chart aggregates per-compound with McLaren-only laps presented as venue-wide 'Compound degradation at Jeddah 2025', 'stint 2' is ignored, and neither team is ever compared or mentioned — while the mock expected a scatter_with_regression team comparison (detector emitted degradation_curve instead).
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: One-directional substring team match drops any team whose canonical name is longer than its colloquial mention; downstream the deg template's driver filter silently narrows a two-team comparison to a single team with no caveat.
member_titles: Team matching requires the message to contain the full canonical team_name — 'Red Bull' never matches 'Red Bull Racing', so team-vs-team prompts silently collapse to one team
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Fully reproduced. chatRuntime/resolution.ts scoreDriverCandidate uses one-directional normalizedMessage.includes(teamName). capture_1/M11.json runtime.resolution shows driverCandidates = Norris #4 and Piastri #81 only, both matchedOn ['team_name'], selectedDriverNumbers [4,81]; the executed SQL contains driver_number IN (4, 81); no Red Bull driver resolved despite the prompt naming Red Bull. The failure itself proves the canonical team string is longer than 'red bull' (the message contains 'red bull', so team_name='red bull' would have matched), and the resolver-disambiguation.test.mjs fixture confirms canonical team_name 'Red Bull Racing'. The answer then presents McLaren-only laps as venue-wide compound degradation, never comparing or mentioning either team, and 'stint 2' is dropped - no caveat anywhere. Design lens: no test, comment, or rubric documents partial-team-mention as unsupported; the silent collapse is not a decision. Severity adjusted P2->P1: team-vs-team comparison prompts using common colloquial names (Red Bull, and any team whose canonical name exceeds its mention) silently misresolve into a single-team answer that misrepresents its scope - a broken feature for a frequent F1 query class, though only one audited prompt hit it.

confirmed.title: inter_to_slick_crossover computed as the FIRST inter-to-slick transition with no wet-phase context, conflating early tyre gambles with the drying-phase crossover and fabricating gamble narratives
confirmed.evidence:
capture_0/s303_wet_crossover#1.json (Silverstone 9947): Antonelli metric 'crossover: Lap 3' (emphasis=true) and answer 'came on Russell lap 39, Antonelli lap 3'. But Antonelli's compound sequence is Int 1-2 -> Hard 3-9 -> Int 10-23 and his wet-flagged laps are 1-2 and 10-23: the lap-3 switch happened BEFORE the wet phase (Russell's wet laps are 11-38), and Antonelli retired on lap 23 still on inters — he never made a drying-phase crossover at all. The takeaway asserts 'Crossover laps differ by 36 laps — one car gambled on the dry line earlier', a fabricated strategy narrative. wetCrossoverInsight.ts line 107 emits the 'gambled' line whenever the spread exceeds 2 laps with no sanity check that the crossover follows the wet phase.
---
s303 (Silverstone 2025): answer states 'crossover came on Russell lap 39, Antonelli lap 3'. Rows show the wet phase ran ~laps 10-38 (wet_track=0 for laps 1-9; markers 'Russell → Int' x=11, 'Antonelli → Int' x=10; 'Russell → Hard' x=39). Antonelli's inter_to_slick_crossover_lap=3 comes from his opening gamble (INTERMEDIATE laps 1-2 on a dry track → HARD lap 3) — he retired at lap 23 and never made the actual drying-phase crossover. Presenting lap 3 and lap 39 as the same kind of event in one sentence is misleading; the two laps are different phenomena.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: The warehouse computation behind inter_to_slick_crossover_lap takes the first INTERMEDIATE-to-slick compound transition in the session rather than the transition that ends the final wet phase; the insight builder then narrates any spread as an early dry-line gamble without validating crossover_lap > first wet-flagged lap.
member_titles: inter_to_slick_crossover_lap picks the FIRST inter-to-slick switch ever, not the drying-phase crossover — fabricates a 36-lap 'gamble' narrative
Per-driver 'inter-to-slick crossover' picks any first inter→slick transition, conflating a lap-3 tyre gamble with the lap-39 drying-phase crossover
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Verified against capture_0/s303_wet_crossover#1.json. Antonelli's 23 rows all carry inter_to_slick_crossover_lap=3; his compound sequence is Int 1-2 -> Hard 3-9 -> Int 10-23 and he retired lap 23 still on inters, while the main wet phase (Russell's wet-flagged laps) ran 11-38 with Russell's crossover at 39 — so the lap-3 value is an opening-phase tyre call, not the drying-phase crossover, and Antonelli never made one. The card nevertheless emits 'Antonelli crossover: Lap 3' with emphasis and the takeaway 'Crossover laps differ by 36 laps — one car gambled on the dry line earlier'; wetCrossoverInsight.ts confirms the gamble line fires purely on crossoverSpread > 2 with no check that the crossover follows the wet phase, and the value is consumed precomputed from analytics.weather_impact (wetCrossover.ts:49). Design lens: the signed-off 'wet-crossover model-inferred, stated on the card' decision covers provenance (the card does carry that caveat) but not this semantic conflation or the fabricated gamble narrative — a genuinely new aspect. Severity kept at P2: the headline framing is misleading for the exact question asked, but the same card's tyre-sequence takeaways expose the true sequences, the scenario is narrow (wet race + early gamble + retirement), and one nuance softens it further — laps 1-2 were damp, so the lap-3 switch was itself a real inter->slick transition, making this a mislabel/conflation rather than fabricated data.

confirmed.title: analytics.corner_analysis misplaced/sparse entry-apex sampling yields phantom brake zones; the plausibility filter lives only in the answer builder, not the chart detector, so chart and text diverge
confirmed.evidence:
Sakhir (M05 and s303, session 10014): zones picked by 'AVG(entry_speed_kph - apex_min_speed_kph) DESC LIMIT 3' (brakeZones.ts lines 43-56) are Turn 8 (avg drop 150.1/128.4 kph), Turn 10 (145.8/125.1) and Turn 1 (5.8/10.4). Real Sakhir Turn 1 is the track's heaviest stop (~330 to ~65 kph); the sampled rows show entry==apex (M05: Norris entry 105.0 apex 105.0 drop 0.0; s303: Russell 105.0/105.0 drop 0.0, Leclerc 114/102 drop 12) — the entry sample is taken after braking is complete. A corner with a 5.8 kph field-average drop ranking third-heaviest also means the genuine remaining brake zones (T4, T11, T13/14) are missing or NULL-filtered in corner_analysis. Spa (s101, session 9939): picked Stavelot (95.5), Pouhon (32.0), Eau Rouge (12.6) — La Source, Les Combes and the Bus Stop, Spa's actual heaviest brake zones, are absent, and Eau Rouge is near flat-out. Downstream, the corrupted zone feeds the headline: M05's '8.0 km/h average across the zones' includes Turn 1's -10 kph delta because brakeZonesInsight.ts's plausibility filter (lines 71-94) only rejects apex<40 kph or entry-ratio<0.6 with apex divergence>8 — a symmetric drop~0 zone passes; s101's Eau Rouge only got caveated because Russell's apex read 0.
---
s101 rows for Eau Rouge: Russell entry_speed_kph 5.0 / apex_min_speed_kph 0.0 and Piastri entry 41.0 / apex 37.0 (lap-1 sample corruption). buildBrakeZonesInsight (synthesis/brakeZonesInsight.ts:75,153,165,176) flags it: metric 'Eau Rouge ⚠ +37.0 km/h — implausible lap-1 sample — excluded from the average', and answer/verdict compute +16.5 km/h from only the 2 usable zones. But the brake_zone_delta detector spec plots series values [-41, 8, 37]: Eau Rouge is the LARGEST bar ('Piastri faster' +37) and the track-map corner window is highlighted in Piastri's color with leader 'Oscar PIASTRI' — visually contradicting the card's own exclusion.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: analytics.corner_analysis has misplaced sampling windows for some corners (entry sampled at/after the apex, giving drop~0) and sparse/NULL coverage for others, so ranking by observed avg drop selects phantom 'heaviest brake zones'; the insight-layer plausibility filter checks apex speed and entry ratio but never validates that a selected zone shows a plausible braking event at all (e.g. zone_avg_drop above a floor).
member_titles: 'Three heaviest brake zones' selection returns non-brake-zones due to corrupted/sparse analytics.corner_analysis entry/apex samples
Brake-zone chart plots a corrupted lap-1 sample as the biggest bar while the card text excludes it
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced. zone_avg_drop_kph values match exactly: M05 Sakhir Turn 8=150.1/Turn 10=145.8/Turn 1=5.8; s303 128.4/125.1/10.4; s101 Spa Stavelot 95.5/Pouhon 32.0/Eau Rouge 12.6. Turn 1 rows show entry==apex (Norris 105/105 drop 0; Russell 105/105) yet it ranks third-heaviest, and M05's '8.0 km/h average' headline includes Turn 1's -10 delta because the brakeZonesInsight.ts filter (apex<40 OR entry-ratio<0.6 with apex divergence>8) passes a symmetric drop~0 zone - all confirmed in code. s101 Eau Rouge rows (Russell 5/0, Piastri 41/37) confirmed, with real heavy zones (La Source etc.) absent. brakeZoneDeltaDetector (registry.ts:1248+) plots every zone with no plausibility filter, so the chart bars/corner-zone leader coloring include the zone the text excludes. Design lens nuance: brakeZonesInsight.ts explicitly documents that suspect zones are 'flagged and kept on the chart but excluded from the average' - so the chart/text split for FLAGGED zones is deliberate, materially weakening the second member. But the primary member - phantom zone selection from corrupted/sparse corner_analysis sampling (drop~0 corner ranked heaviest, genuine zones missing) passing the plausibility filter unflagged into the headline average - is real, unticketed, and not covered by the M05 diverging-encoding sign-off. P2 stands: quality erosion; the pace verdict on each card remains correct.

confirmed.title: core.stint_summary passes through fragmented same-compound stint rows; all consumers equate stint boundaries with pit stops (verdict computed before micro-stint classification), inflating stop counts
confirmed.evidence:
capture_0/s202_strategy_split#1.json (Montreal 9963, Albon vs Sainz): Sainz rows include stint 3 (lap 66-66, MEDIUM, avg_valid_lap NULL), stint 4 (lap 67-67, MEDIUM, NULL) and stint 5 (lap 68-69, MEDIUM, NULL) — three consecutive same-compound 1-2-lap 'stints' implying pit stops on laps 65, 66 and 67 in a row, which is stint-record fragmentation, not strategy. strategySplitInsight.ts itself detects this (lines 206-218) and the captured takeaway says 'Late micro-stints — Sainz stint 3 (1 lap), Sainz stint 4 (1 lap), Sainz stint 5 (2 laps) — point to safety-car or red-flag tyre changes rather than planned strategy'. Yet stops are still computed as stints.length-1 (line 111), so the verdict is YES with summary "Albon ran 1 stop against Sainz's 4", metrics show 'Sainz stops: 4', and the answer asserts 'That is a genuine strategy split: Albon made 1 stop to Sainz's 4.' Excluding the micro-stints both drivers made 1 stop — the real split is compound-order only, so the headline verdict is contradicted by the builder's own classification.
---
s202_strategy_split rows (session 9963, Montreal 2025) show Sainz stints: 1 HARD laps 1-56, 2 MEDIUM 57-65, then three fragments all MEDIUM — stint 3 laps 66-66, stint 4 laps 67-67, stint 5 laps 68-69 — each with avg_valid_lap null. The verdict states as fact: 'Albon made 1 stop to Sainz's 4... stops lap 56, 65, 66, 67' and the stint_gantt draws three 1-2-lap slivers. Three pit stops in laps 65-67 onto the same compound with zero valid laps is the classic raw.stints fragmentation artifact (end-of-race SC), not real strategy. Neither the template SQL (SELECT DISTINCT straight from core.stint_summary), the answerSanity module (pitStints.ts summarize* helpers count every stint row), nor the gantt detector merges consecutive same-compound stints or cross-checks against raw.pit event count.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: core.stint_summary passes through fragmented raw.stints records (repeated same-compound stint rows late in SC-ended races), and strategySplitInsight.ts computes the stop count and verdict BEFORE applying its micro-stint classification, so the disclaimer lands in a takeaway while the verdict, metrics and answer keep the inflated count.

confirmed.title: degradationCurveInsight cliff detection lacks sustained-elevation/plausibility bounds and the takeaway hardcodes a '+' prefix, producing self-contradictory cliff verdicts and malformed '+-22.87s' output
confirmed.evidence:
s101_deg_curve#1.json HARD curve: age0 -4.696s, age1 +12.786s, age2 +8.707s, then a wall of -20s to -22.9s from age 3 through 67. Answer: 'Hard crosses the cliff threshold around age 1 and stays high (-22.87s by age 67)' — the endpoint it cites is 22.9s BELOW baseline, i.e. it did not stay high. Takeaway renders 'Hard: degrades ~-0.341s/lap with a cliff around tyre age 1 (median +-22.87s by age 67)' — negative rate labelled 'degrades' and unconditional '+' prefix producing '+-22.87s' (degradationCurveInsight.ts:93). Cliff detector (lines 66-73) fires on the age-1 spike because it only checks the single next point (>0.4s), never that the curve stays elevated. Root data cause: the fresh-tyre baseline (age<=2 median, degradationCurve.ts:69-74) has no neutralization/SC filter, and Monaco 2025 hard-starters' opening laps were ~20s slow (traffic/SC), poisoning every later delta. Bahrain (R02/s303) numbers check out fine, so this is venue-conditional. The signed-off 'not fuel-corrected' caveat does not cover a 20s SC artifact presented as a tyre cliff at age 1.
---
Monaco card takeaway: 'Hard: degrades ~-0.341s/lap with a cliff around tyre age 1 (median +-22.87s by age 67)' — degradationCurveInsight.ts:96 formats the endpoint as `median +${s.lastDelta.toFixed(2)}s`, hardcoding '+' so negative deltas render '+-22.87s'. Answer text: 'Hard crosses the cliff threshold around age 1 and stays high (-22.87s by age 67)' — 'stays high' paired with a 22.87s-FASTER endpoint. The cliff detector (line 65-74) fires on the first age above +0.6s (Monaco age-1 was +12.8s of SC noise) and never re-checks that the curve stays elevated beyond the next point, and there is no |delta| magnitude guard (anything beyond ~5s is physically not tyre degradation) to trigger a disrupted-race caveat or suppress the cliff claim.
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: Cliff detection lacks a sustained-elevation check across the remaining curve, the 'stays high'/'+' text templates assume positive endpoints, and the template SQL's fresh baseline includes neutralized laps at SC-heavy venues.
member_titles: Monaco deg curve: 'crosses the cliff at age 1 and stays high (-22.87s by age 67)' — self-contradictory cliff narrative from SC-contaminated baseline, plus '+-22.87s' malformed sign
Degradation insight asserts a 'cliff' with a negative endpoint and prints '+-22.87s' (hardcoded + prefix), with no sanity guard for pathological delta magnitudes
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Reproduced in capture_3/s101_deg_curve#1.json (Monaco 9979): HARD rows show age0 -4.696s, age1 +12.786s, age2 +8.707s, then -21.05 to -22.873s from age 3 through 67 against a 98.365s baseline (~22s slower than genuine race pace, consistent with a neutralized opening). Answer: 'Hard crosses the cliff threshold around age 1 and stays high (-22.87s by age 67)' — self-contradictory as claimed. Takeaway renders 'degrades ~-0.341s/lap with a cliff around tyre age 1 (median +-22.87s by age 67)' — malformed '+-' confirmed. Code confirms all three mechanisms: degradationCurveInsight.ts:69 fires the cliff when a point exceeds CLIFF_THRESHOLD_S=0.6 and only the SINGLE next sampled point stays above 2/3 of the threshold (age1 12.786 / age2 8.707 both qualify; the subsequent -21s wall is never checked); line 93 hardcodes `median +${s.lastDelta.toFixed(2)}s`; line 119 hardcodes 'stays high (${endpoint})'; and the cliff branch takes precedence over the fuel-dominated branch, so a negative slope is still labelled 'degrades'. degradationCurve.ts baseline (age<=2 median) has no neutralization/SC filter. Design lens: the 'NOT fuel-corrected' caveat and heavy-fuel-baseline note are on the card and partially soften the damage, and the sustained-check plus fuel-dominated branch show the failure class was considered — but no test, comment, or signed-off decision covers a +12.8s spike being reported as a tyre cliff or the '+-' formatting. Venue-conditional (Bahrain captures are clean). P2 confirmed: self-contradictory narrative and malformed text on one venue, mitigated by extensive in-answer caveats.

confirmed.title: Radar drops axes when EITHER driver is zero (sign-off was BOTH) and the lede claims those axes 'read 0.0' while one driver has a real score, with delta sign attributed regardless of named driver

s303 rows: Verstappen traffic_handling_axis=80.1 (Leclerc 0.0) — axis dropped and the lede claims 'the... traffic... axes read 0.0 in the 2025 score model (not yet populated)', which is false for Verstappen. s101 rows: Piastri traffic=18.4 and Leclerc error_rate=80.0 both dropped the same way ('...traffic and overtaking and consistency axes read 0.0'), leaving 'Among the 1 populated axes'. performanceRadarInsight.ts:64 sets unpopulated when 'a === 0 || b === 0'; the signed-off design decision is drop only when the axis reads 0.0 for BOTH drivers. The key_takeaways caveat (line 91: 'not yet populated for at least one driver') is accurate, but the answer lede (lines 104-105) omits the 'for at least one driver' qualifier and asserts the axes read 0.0 outright.
---
M17 rows: Verstappen traffic_handling_axis=80.1 vs Norris 0.0, yet the answer says 'the qualifying and race pace and tyre management and traffic and overtaking axes read 0.0 in the 2025 score model (not yet populated)' — flatly contradicted by the 80.1 in the same payload. s202:radar#1: Russell error_rate_axis=60.0 vs Piastri 0.0, answer claims the 'consistency' axis 'reads 0.0' and counts only 1 populated axis. The signed-off design only drops axes reading 0.0 for BOTH drivers; performanceRadarInsight.ts:64 excludes on `a === 0 || b === 0` (the exclusion may be defensible, and the takeaway wording 'not yet populated for at least one driver' is accurate) but the answer lede at line 105 asserts the axis value is 0.0. member_titles: Radar drops axes when EITHER driver reads 0.0 (sign-off was BOTH) and the answer lede falsely says those axes 'read 0.0' when one driver has a real score

(not yet populated)'; s101 drops traffic (Piastri 18.4) and consistency (Leclerc 80.0) the same way leaving 'Among the 1 populated axes'; s202 drops consistency despite Russell 60.0; s303 drops traffic despite Verstappen 80.1. Code confirms every element: performanceRadarInsight.ts sets unpopulated on 'a === 0 || b === 0' (~line 64); the takeaway correctly says 'not yet populated for at least one driver' while the answer lede omits the qualifier and flatly asserts the axes 'read 0.0' — false when one driver has a real score. confirmed.title: Synthesis prompt samples only the first 25 rows of lap-ordered results, so answers whose evidence lives beyond row 25 (pit laps, late-race events) punt despite the answer being in the result set

Any question whose answer lives beyond row 25 (pit laps, late-race events) becomes unanswerable on the LLM/heuristic synthesis path.

confirmed.title: Two parallel pg Pool modules share the global pool key but NUMERIC/BIGINT type parsers are registered only in db/driver.ts, which the chat route never imports — chat SQL returns numbers as JSON strings
confirmed.evidence:
Captured rows carry numeric strings in chart-bearing columns: gap_to_leader_s all-string in every race_trace (698/720/710 rows), deg_delta_s + lap_count + compound_baseline_s all-string in both deg curves, all seven radar axis columns string ('75.0', '18.4'), penalty_seconds string in M15, session_key string in the session listings. pgTypes.setTypeParser(1700/20) lives only in src/lib/db/driver.ts:10-11 (whose own comment warns 'without these parsers... bars render as zero'), but runReadOnlySql (src/lib/queries/execute.ts:1) imports pool from src/lib/db.ts, which never registers parsers; grep shows only /api/track-outline and /api/lap-telemetry import db/driver. mapInsight detectors were hardened with parseFiniteNumber (registry.ts:46-58) precisely because of this, but chart components and synthesis builders outside the detector registry get no such guarantee.
---
Captured rows carry chart-critical numerics as strings: race_trace gap_to_leader_s '5.058' (R01, both over_cut captures), wet_crossover lap_time_s, strategy_split avg_valid_lap '83.151', pace-cliff lap_duration/rolling_avg_prev3/delta_vs_rolling_avg (M20), all brake-zone speeds/fractions (s202_brake_zones), all M03 metrics, and session_key as string in heuristic rows. web/src/lib/db/driver.ts registers pgTypes.setTypeParser(1700/20) with the comment 'chart detectors key off typeof v === number - without these parsers... bars render as zero', but that module is imported ONLY by /api/track-outline and /api/lap-telemetry; runReadOnlySql (web/src/lib/queries/execute.ts) imports pool from web/src/lib/db.ts, which has zero setTypeParser calls. Downstream is only partially hardened: mapInsight/detectors/registry.ts coerces via parseFiniteNumber, and synthesis builders coerce, but legacy builders in web/src/lib/mapInsight.ts lines 298 and 420 still do 'typeof v === "number" ? v : 0', zeroing every string value (grouped_bar corner speeds, line-chart lap-time values).
confirmed.suspected_root_cause: Split pool modules: the numeric type-parser fix was applied to src/lib/db/driver.ts but the chat execution path uses the older src/lib/db.ts pool, so oid 1700 (NUMERIC, e.g. every ROUND(x::numeric,3) projection) and oid 20 (BIGINT/COUNT) are serialized as strings on the chat route; consumers that key off typeof v === 'number' silently drop or zero these values.
member_titles: Chat SQL path returns NUMERIC/BIGINT columns as JSON strings — pg type parsers are registered only in db/driver.ts, which the chat route never imports
Chat SQL path returns NUMERIC/BIGINT as strings - pg type parsers are registered only in db/driver.ts, not in the db.ts pool that runReadOnlySql uses
confirmed.verdict_reasoning: Core facts verified: pgTypes.setTypeParser(1700/20) exists only in src/lib/db/driver.ts (lines 10-11, with the 'bars render as zero' comment); grep confirms only /api/track-outline and /api/lap-telemetry import it, while runReadOnlySql (queries/execute.ts:1) imports pool from src/lib/db.ts which registers no parsers; captures empirically confirm chat-path NUMERIC/BIGINT arriving as strings (R01 gap_to_leader_s '5.058', all seven radar axes '75.0'/'18.4' strings in s101_radar#1, session_key '11436' string in heuristic listings). However the claimed live blast radius is overstated: the string-zeroing consumers cited (mapInsight.ts:298 and :420 'typeof v === "number" ? v : 0') sit in legacy builders that are module-private and unreachable — detectChart (mapInsight.ts:262-267) delegates entirely to the detector registry, which is deliberately hardened with parseFiniteNumber (registry.ts:46-58) whose comment explicitly documents this exact string-form hazard, and synthesis builders coerce via num() helpers. Chart components receive registry-normalized numbers. So no capture shows user-visible zeroed/dropped values from this gap today. Design lens: the registry comment is a documented mitigation of the phenomenon, though nothing blesses the pool split itself, so not fully design-refuted. Downgrading P2 -> P3: real architectural inconsistency and latent hazard for any new non-registry consumer, but present user impact is effectively nil.
